instruction: Apply build-vs-buy reasoning: analyze total cost of ownership, lock-in, and customization for a feature decision.
output:
<think>
Build vs buy decision framework: (1) Core vs commodity: if this feature is your competitive advantage, build it. If it is table stakes (auth, payments, email), buy it. (2) Total Cost of Ownership: buying is not just the license fee — add integration cost (weeks to months), ongoing maintenance (API changes, version upgrades), support cost (tickets, escalations), migration cost (if you need to switch later). Building is not just dev time — add ongoing maintenance, on-call burden, feature development as requirements change. (3) Customization: if you need to customize more than 30 percent of a bought solution, building is usually cheaper long-term. (4) Lock-in risk: proprietary APIs, data formats, contractual terms create switching costs. Mitigate with abstraction layers. (5) Time to market: buying is usually faster initially, but the customization tax grows over time. (6) Team expertise: do you have the skills to build it? If not, buying reduces risk.
</think>

instruction: Reason about vendor lock-in: identify lock-in vectors, switching costs, and mitigation strategies.
output:
<think>
Lock-in vectors: (1) Proprietary APIs — your code calls vendor-specific endpoints, switching means rewriting. (2) Data formats — vendor stores data in proprietary format, export is lossy or slow. (3) Network effects — your users are on the platform, moving them is hard. (4) Contractual — long-term contracts with early termination penalties. (5) Knowledge — your team knows vendor X deeply, retraining is expensive. Mitigation: (1) Abstraction layer — wrap vendor API behind your own interface. When you switch vendors, only the adapter changes. (2) Standard formats — use SQL standard, not vendor extensions. Export data regularly to portable formats. (3) Multi-cloud capability — test deploys on alternative platforms quarterly. (4) Contractual — negotiate shorter terms, data portability clauses. (5) Decision rule: use vendor-specific features only when the value clearly exceeds the lock-in cost. Default to portable, standard approaches.
</think>

instruction: Design a zero-downtime database migration plan from PostgreSQL to a new database system.
output:
<think>
Zero-downtime migration pattern (dual-write): Phase 1 DUAL WRITE — all writes go to both old and new database. New DB is not yet used for reads. Verify write paths work. Phase 2 BACKFILL — copy all historical data from old to new. Verify row counts and checksums match. Phase 3 SHADOW READ — read from both databases, compare results, log discrepancies. Fix any data inconsistencies. Phase 4 SWITCH READS — point read traffic to new database. Old DB still receives writes as safety net. Phase 5 STOP OLD WRITES — disable writes to old database. New DB is now the sole source of truth. Phase 6 DECOMMISSION — shut down old database after verification period. Each phase has a rollback plan (revert the feature flag). Timeline: weeks to months depending on data volume and complexity. Use feature flags at every stage so any phase can be reversed in seconds.
</think>

instruction: Create a technology evaluation framework for assessing new technologies against team needs.
output:
<think>
Technology radar approach with four rings: ADOPT (use in production for new projects), TRIAL (use in non-critical projects to evaluate), ASSESS (research and prototype, not in production), HOLD (do not start new projects with this, migrate away). Evaluation criteria per technology: (1) Team expertise — can we hire for this? Do we have existing skills? (2) Community — size, activity, bus factor (one maintainer is risky). (3) Documentation — quality and coverage. (4) Performance — meets our requirements? (5) Operational complexity — how hard to deploy, monitor, debug? (6) License — compatible with our business model? (7) Maturity — production-proven at our scale? (8) Integration — works with our existing stack? Review quarterly: technologies can move between rings based on experience. Each move requires a brief write-up explaining why.
</think>
